title: Near-boundary unhealthy samples are accumulated as healthy
reasoning: Snapshot health is classified with exact integer math, but the ratio persisted for the next interval is truncated to 6 decimals. updateHealthAccumulators then re-classifies the previous interval by parsing that truncated string and checking <= 1.01. Any exact ratio in (1.01, 1.010001) is correctly emitted as healthBinaryValue 0.000000, but its deviationRatio can truncate to 1.010000 and later be counted as healthy carry time, overstating uptime.
reproduction: A sample with priceDifference=10100001n and effectiveThresholdBps=10000000 produces exact ratio 1.0100001, so computeHealthSnapshotFields marks it unhealthy, but deviationRatio is stored as "1.010000". If that becomes pool.lastDeviationRatio, updateHealthAccumulators treats the following interval as healthy.
recommendation: Do not derive accumulator health from the rounded display ratio. Persist/use the exact binary health decision for the previous sample, or store an integer numerator/threshold decision field and carry that into updateHealthAccumulators.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: The tests cover exactly 1.01 and a much larger just-above case (1.012), but not a value just above 1.01 that truncates back to 1.010000 at 6 decimals before the accumulator re-reads it.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a recordHealthSample/updateHealthAccumulators test for priceDifference=10100001n, threshold=10000000, then assert the next interval adds zero healthBinarySeconds while still adding healthTotalSeconds.

history.note: Health snapshots now preserve exact unhealthy decisions near the 1.01 boundary for accumulator updates; regression added.
